name: Queen Mother of the West
description: The Queen Mother of the West, also known as Xiwangmu (西王母), is a powerful and ancient goddess in Chinese mythology and religion. She reigns over the mythical paradise of Kunlun Mountain, a realm of immortality and abundance. Originally depicted as a fearsome, half-human, half-beast figure associated with plagues and calamities, her image gradually transformed over time into a benevolent and beautiful deity. She is now revered as a guardian of immortality, bestower of blessings, and a symbol of feminine power. Xiwangmu controls the elixir of immortality and the peaches of immortality, which ripen only once every three thousand years. She is said to grant these gifts to worthy individuals, allowing them to achieve eternal life. Her court is attended by immortal maidens and divine beings, and she is often associated with the Eight Immortals. She plays a crucial role in Taoist beliefs and is worshipped by those seeking longevity, health, and prosperity. She is also seen as a protector of women and a source of spiritual guidance. The Queen Mother of the West embodies both the allure and the mystery of the divine feminine in Chinese culture, representing a powerful force of nature and a beacon of hope for eternal life.
subtitle: Goddess of Immortality and Western Paradise

keyMyths.title: The Peaches of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Xiwangmu possesses a garden where peaches of immortality grow, ripening only once every three thousand years. Eating these peaches grants immortality to those deemed worthy.

keyMyths.title: Meeting with Emperor Mu of Zhou
keyMyths.description: Emperor Mu of Zhou journeyed to Kunlun Mountain and met with Xiwangmu, where they enjoyed a feast and discussed matters of state and spirituality. This encounter solidified her importance in imperial lore.

keyMyths.title: Granting Immortality to Chang'e
keyMyths.description: In some versions of the Chang'e myth, Xiwangmu gives Chang'e the elixir of immortality, which she consumes, causing her to float to the moon and become the Moon Goddess.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of the Chang'e myth

sources.author: Werner, E.T.C.
